text.title: Async Support and Hybrid Architecture
text.summary: Async depth per framework, the FastAPI+Django hybrid pattern, and production deployment commands.

rule.statement: For Django async views, all ORM calls must use sync_to_async or the a-prefixed ORM methods (aall, aget, etc.). Not every ORM operation has an async alias — check per Django release.
rule.rationale: Calling sync ORM inside an async view blocks the event loop thread. Django does not detect this automatically.

rule.statement: Flask async views run under WSGI — each request blocks a worker for the coroutine duration. Do not use async views in Flask expecting real concurrency.
rule.rationale: WSGI is synchronous; Flask's async view support wraps the coroutine in a sync call, providing no concurrency benefit.

section.title: FastAPI + Django Hybrid

rule.statement: In a FastAPI+Django hybrid, Django owns the database schema (all migrations run via manage.py). FastAPI reads from the same tables using SQLAlchemy in read-mostly mode.
rule.rationale: Two ORMs writing schema migrations to the same database causes conflicts. Single DDL owner (Django) eliminates them.

section.p: Pattern: FastAPI handles high-throughput public API traffic; Django provides admin panel and content management. Both connect to the same database with clear read/write boundaries.

example.code:
# Django ASGI (requires --lifespan off until Channels configured) gunicorn config.asgi:application -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker -w 4 --lifespan off
# FastAPI uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers 4
# Flask WSGI gunicorn -w 4 -b 0.0.0.0:8000 app:app

rule.statement: Pin one ASGI server per app and one WSGI server per app. Never mix gunicorn workers for different frameworks in the same process.
rule.rationale: LLMs switch to whatever server they saw in the last training example; locking the server in constitution.md prevents this.
